title: Soil Is Alive
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

required.title: [Read Aloud / Look Together] Jump Into Science: Dirt
required.author: Steve Tomecek

required.title: [Field Guide] A magnifying glass and your local soil

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Compost Stew: An A to Z Recipe for the Earth
suggested.author: Mary McKenna Siddals

suggested.title: [Look Together] The Hidden Life of a Toad
suggested.author: Doug Wechsler

supplementary.title: [Video] YouTube: "Soil Food Web" by Dr. Elaine Ingham
supplementary.author: Dr. Elaine Ingham

competence_markers.description: The child can find earthworms in soil and explain what they do (eat dead material, make tunnels, leave castings that feed plants)
The child can identify rough differences between healthy soil (dark, crumbly, smells earthy, has organisms) and depleted soil (pale, hard, no smell, no organisms)
The child actively participates in composting — adding materials, checking the pile, observing changes
The child understands that bare soil is stressed soil and can explain why mulch helps
The child treats soil with respect as a living system, not as inert dirt
The child can name at least three things that live in healthy soil
The child maintains an observation journal or regularly narrates what they observe on the land

content.why_this_matters:
Pick up a handful of dirt from your yard. Now pick up a handful of soil from a garden bed or a forest floor. They look different, they smell different, and they feel different. That is because one of them is alive — really alive — with millions of tiny creatures you cannot see, and the other may be mostly dead.

Soil is not just brown stuff on the ground. It is a living world. There are more living things in a single handful of healthy soil than there are people on Earth. Earthworms, beetles, fungi, bacteria — all of them live in the soil, and all of them work together to make it possible for plants to grow. Without healthy soil, nothing grows. No gardens. No forests. No food.

Every farmer who ever lived understood this. The people who built the great civilizations you are learning about in 1.1 — the Mesopotamians, the Egyptians, the Chinese — all of them knew that their food came from the soil, and that taking care of the soil was the most important thing they could do. When civilizations forgot this, their soil died and their civilizations fell.

You are going to learn what makes soil alive, how to tell if soil is healthy, and how to start making it healthier. This is where everything else in 2.5 begins.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Direct sensory experience with soil** — the child handles, smells, and observes soil from multiple environments and begins to distinguish healthy from depleted soil
2. **Understanding that soil is alive** — the concept that soil contains a community of living organisms, not inert material
3. **Earthworm and soil life awareness** — the child finds and observes soil organisms and understands their role
4. **Composting fundamentals** — participation in building, maintaining, and observing a compost system
5. **Observation as a primary skill** — the habit of watching the land carefully and noticing what grows where and why
6. **The concept that bare soil is stressed soil** — mulching, ground cover, and protecting the earth
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Is Soil Made Of?

Soil is made of four things mixed together:

1. **Tiny pieces of rock** — sand (big pieces you can feel), silt (smaller, smooth), and clay (so tiny you cannot see individual pieces). These come from rocks that have been broken down over thousands and millions of years by water, wind, ice, and the roots of plants.
2. **Dead plant and animal material** — leaves that fell, roots that died, insects that lived and died in the ground. This is called organic matter, and it is the most important part of healthy soil.
3. **Living things** — earthworms, insects, fungi, bacteria, and many other creatures too small to see. These are the soil's workforce. They break down the dead material and turn it into food for plants.
4. **Air and water** — healthy soil has spaces between its particles where air and water can move. Plants' roots need both air and water to survive. Soil that is packed too tight (compacted) has no room for air or water, and plants struggle.

**Your first activity:** Go outside and collect small handfuls of soil from three different places — under a tree, from a garden bed, from a bare patch of ground, from a path where people walk. Put each one on a white plate or piece of paper. Look at them. Smell them. Feel them between your fingers. How are they different? Which one is darkest? Which one smells the richest? Which one crumbles easiest? The dark, crumbly, earthy-smelling one is the healthiest. You are already learning to read soil.

### Part 2: The Creatures in the Soil

If you dig into healthy soil, you will find living things. Lots of them.

**Earthworms** are the ones you will find first. An earthworm eats soil. It swallows dead leaves and tiny pieces of organic material, digests them, and leaves behind castings — worm poop — that are some of the richest plant food on Earth. Earthworms also dig tunnels through the soil as they move, and those tunnels let water and air reach plant roots. A garden full of earthworms is a healthy garden.

**Beetles, grubs, and insects** live in the soil too. Some of them eat dead plant material and help break it down. Some of them eat other insects. Together they are part of the community that keeps soil alive.

**Fungi** — you might see thin white threads in the soil if you look carefully, especially under rotting wood or leaf piles. These threads are fungi, and they do something extraordinary: they connect plants to each other underground. A fungal network can stretch across an entire forest, carrying water and nutrients from one plant to another. Scientists call this the "wood wide web."

**Bacteria** — you cannot see these, but they are there by the billions. Some bacteria break down dead material. Some convert nitrogen from the air into a form that plants can use — this is called nitrogen fixation, and it is one of the most important processes in all of agriculture.

**Your second activity:** Go outside with a shovel and dig into garden soil or forest floor soil. How many earthworms can you find in one shovel-full? What else do you see? Beetles? Grubs? White fungal threads? Centipedes? Count what you find and record it. A shovel-full of healthy soil should have several earthworms. A shovel-full of dead, compacted soil may have none.

### Part 3: Compost — Turning Scraps into Soil

Composting is one of the most remarkable things you can learn. You take food scraps, leaves, grass clippings, and other things that would normally be thrown away, pile them up together, and living things — bacteria, fungi, worms — break them all down into rich, dark, beautiful material that makes plants grow. It is recycling at the deepest level: turning waste into food for the soil.

**What goes in a compost pile:**
- Fruit and vegetable scraps from the kitchen
- Eggshells
- Coffee grounds and tea leaves
- Leaves, grass clippings, straw
- Small sticks and twigs
- Shredded cardboard and paper (in small amounts)

**What does NOT go in a compost pile:**
- Meat, bones, or dairy (these attract animals and smell bad)
- Pet waste
- Diseased plants
- Weeds that have gone to seed

**How it works:** The pile needs to be kept moist — like a wrung-out sponge. It needs to be turned occasionally so air can get in. Over weeks and months, the material breaks down. When it is finished, it looks like dark, crumbly earth and smells like the forest floor after rain. That smell is the smell of healthy biology at work.

**Your third activity:** Help build a compost pile or bin. Collect kitchen scraps for a week. Add leaves or straw. Water it lightly. Over the next several weeks, check on it regularly. What is happening? Is it getting warm inside? (A working compost pile heats up because the bacteria are working so hard they generate heat.) Are there worms in it? What does it smell like? This is your first experience as a soil builder.

### Part 4: Bare Soil Is Stressed Soil

Walk around your property or neighborhood. Notice where the ground is bare — no plants, no leaves, no covering. Now notice where the ground is covered with plants, mulch, or leaf litter. The bare spots are where the soil is struggling.

Here is why: when rain falls on bare soil, it hits the surface directly and washes away the topsoil — the best, most fertile layer. This is called erosion. When the sun beats down on bare soil, it dries out and gets hot, which kills the bacteria and fungi that live near the surface. When wind blows across bare soil, it carries the fine particles away.

**Covered soil** is protected. Mulch — leaves, wood chips, straw spread on the surface — shields the soil from rain, sun, and wind. It holds moisture in. It keeps the soil cooler in summer and warmer in winter. And as the mulch slowly breaks down, it feeds the soil organisms underneath.

**Your fourth activity:** Help spread mulch around plants in your garden or around a tree. Use leaves, wood chips, or straw. Spread it a few inches thick, but keep it away from the trunk of the tree or the stem of the plant. Check under the mulch after one week, then after one month. What do you find underneath? (You should find moisture, earthworms, and the beginning of decomposition. The soil under mulch is alive in a way that bare soil is not.)

### Part 5: Watching the Land Through the Seasons

The soil and the land do not stay the same all year. In spring, the ground thaws and earthworms become active. Plants push through and begin to grow. In summer, the soil is warm and bacteria are working hard — this is when compost breaks down fastest. In fall, leaves drop and begin to cover the ground — nature's own mulch. In winter, the soil rests, but the organisms are still there, just slower.

Your job is to watch. Pick one spot — a garden bed, a patch of forest floor, an area near your compost pile — and observe it through the year. What changes? When do the worms come out? When do the leaves break down? What happens to the soil after a heavy rain? What happens during a dry spell?

This watching is the most important skill in all of land work. The person who watches carefully and notices what is happening will always make better decisions than the person who follows instructions without looking.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Soil Comparison and Identification
**Developing well:** The child picks up soil and immediately notices qualities — color, texture, smell, moisture, organisms. They can explain in their own words why one sample seems healthier than another. They use phrases like "this one has worms" or "this one smells like the forest."

**Developing slowly:** The child handles soil but cannot articulate differences. All soil looks "the same" to them. They show no curiosity about what they are holding.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have they handled enough different soil types? A child who has only touched one kind of soil has no basis for comparison. Take them to a forest, a construction site, a garden, a clay bank — the contrasts teach.

### 2. Earthworm and Organism Awareness
**Developing well:** The child actively looks for soil organisms during any digging activity. They can explain what earthworms do. They are curious about other organisms they find and want to identify them.

**Developing slowly:** The child is indifferent to or disgusted by soil organisms. They do not look for them unless prompted.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is disgust the issue? Some children need time to get comfortable with worms and insects. Start with observation (magnifying glass, not touching) and progress to gentle handling. Do not force it — let curiosity develop naturally. If the child is genuinely squeamish, observe organisms without handling until comfort develops.

### 3. Composting Participation
**Developing well:** The child adds materials to the compost without prompting, checks on the pile regularly, and can describe what is happening inside (getting warm, breaking down, worms moving in). They understand the basic concept that scraps become soil food.

**Developing slowly:** The child forgets about the compost pile unless reminded. They can recite what goes in but show no interest in what is happening over time.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the compost pile accessible and visible? A pile in the back corner that requires effort to reach gets forgotten. Place it where the child passes it daily. Make adding scraps part of a kitchen routine — after dinner, scraps go to the compost. Routine builds habit.

### 4. Mulch and Bare Soil Understanding
**Developing well:** The child can explain why bare soil is a problem and why mulch helps. They have observed the difference between mulched and bare ground in their own yard and can describe what they found.

**Developing slowly:** The child can repeat "bare soil is bad" but has not internalized it through observation. They have not checked under mulch or compared areas.

**Diagnostic questions:** Did you actually do the mulch comparison activity? If not, this is your gap. The child needs to feel the moist soil under mulch and the dry, hard soil next to it. The contrast is the lesson.

### 5. Observation Habit
**Developing well:** The child looks at the land with curiosity. They notice things unprompted — "the worms are out because it rained" or "the compost is steaming today." Their observation journal has regular entries showing change over time.

**Developing slowly:** The child does not notice their environment unless directed. Observation entries are sporadic and lack detail.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling observation? If you walk past a garden bed without looking at it, so will the child. Point out what you notice: "Look how the plants are taller where the compost was added." "Feel how cool the soil is under this leaf pile." Your noticing teaches them to notice.
